gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for curry udon in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: udon noodles (white wheat, boiled), which have a reported GI of approximately 55 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The curry sauce and vegetables (carrot, potato) contribute additional starch but also fibre, which moderates the overall glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 46.0) / 100 = 25.3, based on 400 g serving with 11.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content of 282 mg per 100 g; a 400 g bowl delivers approximately 1128 mg sodium, representing over 49% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, avoid drinking the full curry broth, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake from a single meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined udon noodles have a medium-to-high GI (~55), and a standard 400 g serving delivers approximately 46 g of carbohydrate with only 4.8 g of dietary fibre, producing a high glycaemic load of ~25.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce noodle portion size, pair with a protein source such as tofu or egg to slow gastric emptying, and request curry broth made from curry powder rather than starch-thickened roux to lower the glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium load per serving (approximately 1128 mg per 400 g bowl) and unknown saturated fat content from curry roux are the primary cardiovascular concerns.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, avoid consuming the full broth, and choose curry preparations made with minimal oil and no cream to reduce saturated fat and sodium exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Curry udon is a Japanese noodle dish consisting of thick, chewy wheat-based udon noodles served in a rich Japanese curry sauce, typically containing carrot and potato. The dish is a fusion of Japanese curry — a mild, sweet-savoury curry made from a roux-based curry block — and the traditional udon noodle soup format. The broth is thicker and more viscous than standard udon soup, with a warm, mildly spiced flavour profile. Curry udon is widely consumed in Japan and has become popular across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore where it appears in Japanese restaurant menus. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 73 kcal, 2.49 g protein, 1.9 g fat, and 11.5 g carbohydrate with 1.19 g dietary fibre and 282 mg sodium. The dish is moderate in calories but high in sodium per serving, and the refined udon noodles produce a medium glycaemic index of approximately 55. It is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes without portion modification, and sodium management is important for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains wheat and gluten allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060040) with a confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved source (University of Sydney GI Database, udon noodles) and clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact curry udon match. Calcium (9.0 mg/100g) and iron (0.41 mg/100g) are reported by HPB and included. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on boiled udon noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for curry udon exists in approved sources. The input record classified this dish under 'mamak' cuisine, which is inconsistent with the dish description (Japanese curry udon); cuisine has been corrected to Japanese. Dietary flags: halal status is uncertain as Japanese curry roux may contain pork-derived ingredients; set to false pending verification. Vegetarian status is uncertain as dashi broth typically contains fish (katsuobushi); set to false.
